snp2.12:1.2: Thus have I heard. On one occasion the Blessed One was dwelling at Āḷavī, at the Aggāḷava Cetiya. Now on that occasion the Venerable Vaṅgīsa’s preceptor, the Elder Nigrodhakappa, had recently attained final nibbāna at the Aggāḷava Cetiya. Then, while the Venerable Vaṅgīsa was alone in seclusion, the following reflection arose in his mind: “Did my preceptor attain final nibbāna or not?”
snp2.12:1.3: In the evening, when he emerged from seclusion, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa approached the Blessed One, paid homage to him, and sat down to one side. [60] Seated to one side, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa said to the Blessed One: “Here, Bhante, while I was alone in seclusion, the following reflection arose in my mind: ‘Did my preceptor attain final nibbāna or not?’”
snp2.12:1.4: Then, having risen from his seat, he arranged his upper robe over one shoulder, reverentially saluted the Blessed One, and addressed the Blessed One in verse:
snp2.12:2.2:
343
“We ask
the teacher of perfect wisdom,
the cutter-off of doubts in this very life:
a bhikkhu passed away at Aggāḷava,
one well known, famous, inwardly quenched. (1)
snp2.12:2.3:
344
“Nigrodhakappa was his name,
which you, Blessed One, gave to the brahmin.
Energetic, he lived intent on release,
paying homage to you, the one who shows the durable. (2)
snp2.12:2.4:
345
“O Sakya, we too all wish to know
about that disciple, O universal eye.
Fully attentive, our ears are ready to hear;
you are our teacher; you are unsurpassed. (3)
snp2.12:2.5:
346
“Cut off our doubt! Tell me this:
announce he attained nibbāna, you of broad wisdom.
Speak up in our midst, O universal eye,
like thousand-eyed Sakka among the devas. (4)
snp2.12:2.7:
348
“If no man would ever dispel the defilements
as a wind might dispel a mass of clouds,
the entire world would be enshrouded, utter darkness,
and even brilliant men would not shine. (6)
snp2.12:2.8:
349
“But wise men are makers of light.
I consider you, O hero,
to be such a one.
Knowing, we come to one endowed with insight:
in the assemblies, disclose to us [the fate of] Kappa. (7)
snp2.12:2.9:
350
“Quickly send forth your lovely voice, O lovely one!
Like a goose that has stretched out, gently honk
with your rounded voice, well-modulated.
We are all upright: let us hear you! (8)
snp2.12:2.10:
351
“He has abandoned birth and death entirely;
having pressed the pure one, I will make him speak the Dhamma.
For worldlings cannot act on their wishes,
but for the tathāgatas, action is based on comprehension. (9)
snp2.12:2.11:
352
“This excellent declaration of yours
has been accepted [as coming] from one of upright wisdom.
This last reverential salutation is offered.
Knowing, do not delude us, you of perfect wisdom. (10)
snp2.12:2.12:
353
“Having understood the noble Dhamma from top to bottom,
knowing, do not delude us, O one of perfect energy. [62]
As one afflicted by heat in summer longs for water,
I long for your word: so pour down the sound!
(11)
snp2.12:2.13:
354
“Was the beneficial spiritual life
that Kappāyana led not lived in vain by him?
Did he attain nibbāna or did a residue remain?
Let us hear just how he was liberated.” (12)
snp2.12:3.3:
355
“He cut off craving here for name-and-form,”
“the stream of the Dark One that had long lay dormant.
He crossed over birth and death entirely.”
Thus spoke the Blessed One, chief of the five. (13)
snp2.12:3.4:
356
“Having heard your word,
I am pleased, O best of rishis.
Indeed, my question was not in vain;
the brahmin did not deceive me. (14)
snp2.12:3.5:
357
“The Buddha’s disciple was one
who acted in accordance with what he said.
He cut through the net of Death,
extended tightly by the Deceiver. (15)
snp2.12:3.6:
358
“Kappiya, O Blessed One,
saw the origin of clinging.
Kappāyana, indeed, overcame
the domain of Death so hard to cross.” (16) [63]
